planting.note: Plant rhizomes in early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Zantedeschia aethiopica is considerably more cold-hardy than hybrid calla lilies, tolerating temperatures to about 10–15°F when the rhizomes are mulched — reliably perennial in zone 7–10 without digging. In zone 9 it grows semi-evergreen to evergreen through mild winters. Thrives in consistently moist to wet soil; it grows naturally in stream banks and wetland margins and tolerates shallow standing water. The large, glossy, arrowhead-shaped leaves are attractive even when not in bloom. Provides exceptional winter and spring interest in zone 9 — blooming January through April when little else is flowering in shaded, wet garden areas.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Green Goddess Calla Lily actually thrives in consistently wet or waterlogged conditions and is one of the few ornamental bulbs that can grow in shallow water; Green Goddess is a distinctive cultivar of Common Calla Lily (Zantedeschia aethiopica) producing unusual, large spathes that are primarily green with white interior patterning — the green coloration of the spathe creates a sophisticated, refined floral effect quite different from the pure white flowers of standard calla lilies. The combination of large, bold, deep green arrowhead leaves and the green-and-white flowers from winter through spring creates one of the most elegant combinations of foliage and flower available for moist, partially shaded zone 9 gardens. Standard calla lily (Z. aethiopica) is one of the most cold-hardy species in the genus — far more winter-hardy than the hybrid colored calla lilies that dominate the commercial market — and reliably perennial in zone 7 without the digging and indoor storage required for hybrid callas. For zone 9 gardeners with wet, shaded areas — pond edges, stream banks, low-lying wet depressions, north-facing beds with poor drainage — Green Goddess Calla provides one of the most ornamentally effective solutions available for these challenging conditions.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Zantedeschia Species for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Hardy Calla Lily for Gulf South
North Carolina State University Extension — Zantedeschia aethiopica in Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall (September–November in zone 9) for best establishment before spring bloom; spring planting is possible but produces weaker first-year plants. Black Gamecock Louisiana Iris blooms in April–May in zone 9 — the peak of spring when the large, fleur-de-lis-form flowers create one of the most spectacular displays available in moist, native garden settings. Louisiana irises thrive in the moist to wet, acidic soil conditions common in the Gulf South; they naturally grow in swamps, bayous, and stream margins. Divide clumps every 4–5 years when blooming declines. Louisiana iris culture in the Gulf South is supported by the Society for Louisiana Irises, which maintains extensive hybridization and evaluation programs.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, fertile, very acidic to slightly acidic soil — Black Gamecock Louisiana Iris and all Louisiana iris species require the acidic, consistently moist to wet soil conditions that prevail in their native Gulf Coast swamp and stream bank habitats; Black Gamecock is one of the most famous and widely grown Louisiana iris cultivars, producing large, flat, velvety flowers in an intense, nearly pure black-violet — one of the deepest, most saturated dark purple flowers available among all iris types — on 2–3 foot stems above the upright, strap-like foliage. Louisiana irises (Iris x louisiana) are complex hybrids among five native species of the Gulf Coast, bred extensively in Louisiana since the early 20th century into thousands of cultivars spanning almost the complete color spectrum. As a group they are the most heat-tolerant and humidity-tolerant irises available — outperforming bearded iris, Japanese iris, and Siberian iris in the sustained summer heat and high humidity of the Gulf South that limits those species' culture. The fall-to-spring growing period of Louisiana irises aligns perfectly with the Gulf South climate: active growth occurs in the mild fall through spring weather, bloom peaks in April–May, and the plants go semi-dormant in summer heat. Black Gamecock's extraordinary dark flower color makes it one of the most impactful single plants in the spring iris display.
sources: Society for Louisiana Irises — Black Gamecock Iris Profile
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Louisiana Iris Culture and Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Louisiana Iris for Florida Landscapes

planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and overwinter indoors. Dieffenbachia is sensitive to temperatures below 55°F and damaged by temperatures below 50°F. Keep out of reach of children and pets — all plant parts contain calcium oxalate crystals that cause severe oral burning, swelling, and temporary speech loss (the source of the common name 'dumb cane'). Maintain consistently moist but not waterlogged soil. Remove lower leaves as they yellow naturally to maintain the cane-like stem display. Large, well-grown specimens create a genuine tropical statement on shaded patios and porches in zone 9 during the warm season.

directions: Plant in shade to bright indirect light — never in direct sun — in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil; Camilla is a Dieffenbachia cultivar with exceptionally pale, near-white central leaf areas surrounded by a narrow dark green margin — creating a particularly bright, luminous effect that makes it one of the most light-reflecting shade foliage plants available. The large, paddle-shaped leaves (up to 12 inches long on mature plants) are produced alternately on an upright, bamboo-like stem, creating the characteristic growth habit of the Dieffenbachia genus — a tropical cane plant whose leaves gradually drop from the lower stem as it grows, exposing the attractive stem below while the leafy canopy develops at the top. As an outdoor container plant for shaded patios and porches in zone 9 summers, Camilla Dumb Cane provides a tropical, lush presence that is difficult to achieve with plants that must remain permanently outdoors in the ground. The combination of very high shade tolerance, large luminous leaves, and adaptability to container culture makes Dieffenbachia one of the most practical tropical foliage plants for creating shaded outdoor living spaces in zone 9 during the warm season.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dieffenbachia Species for Florida Interiors and Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Plants for Gulf South Patios
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Dieffenbachia as Tropical Landscape and Interior Plant

planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain above 55°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and bring indoors before temperatures approach 45°F. Massangeana corn plant is sensitive to fluoride and boron in tap water, which causes leaf tip and edge burn — use filtered water, rainwater, or allow tap water to sit uncovered overnight to dissipate chlorine. In zone 10–11 it can be grown as a permanent landscape tree reaching 15–20 feet. Outdoors in zone 9's summer humidity, the plant grows more rapidly than indoors. The fragrant white to cream flower spikes that appear on mature plants have an intense, sweet fragrance noticeable across a significant distance.

directions: Plant in shade to bright indirect light — never in direct sun, which bleaches and burns the variegated leaves — in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil; allow soil to dry slightly between waterings; Massangeana is the classic variegated corn plant dracaena, producing wide, strap-like leaves with a broad bright yellow to lime-green central stripe on a dark green background — the most distinctive and widely recognized leaf pattern of any tropical foliage plant in the American market. The large leaves (up to 3 feet long and 4 inches wide) are produced in rosettes at the tips of the thick, upright, cane-like stem — a growth form that naturally produces the same architectural, palm-tree-like effect whether grown as a 2-foot houseplant or a 15-foot landscape tree in tropical zones. Corn plant is among the very best shade and low-light tropical foliage plants for zone 9 outdoor display — its tolerance of low light conditions, relative drought tolerance, and ability to go without watering for extended periods makes it practical as an outdoor container specimen on covered patios and porches where access to the plant for watering may be irregular. The plant's ability to shed its lower leaves and expose the attractive, bamboo-like stem as it grows creates an evolving form over years that becomes increasingly interesting as the plant matures.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dracaena fragrans for Florida Landscapes and Interiors
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage for Gulf South Indoor-Outdoor Culture
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Dracaena Species for Tropical Landscape Use

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Pink Autumn Sage is among the most cold-hardy of the Salvia greggii selections, with robust performance across zones 7–9. Prune plants back to 6–8 inches in late winter to remove winter-killed stems and stimulate dense, floriferous new growth. In zone 9 blooms most heavily spring (March–May) and fall (September–November) with reduced but continuous bloom through summer. The soft pink flowers attract hummingbirds and butterflies through both bloom peaks.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil — Pink Autumn Sage thrives in dry, rocky, calcareous conditions and is one of the most drought-tolerant flowering perennials for zones 7–9; Pink is the soft pink-flowered form of Autumn Sage (Salvia greggii), producing the same compact evergreen shrub habit and nearly continuous bloom from spring through fall as the red-flowered form but in a softer, more pastel warm pink that complements a broader range of garden color palettes. The pale to medium pink flowers (slightly larger than the red form on some selections) provide a refined alternative to the vivid scarlet that is the standard Salvia greggii color — useful for gardeners who prefer harmonious soft-toned plantings over vivid red accent plants. Like all S. greggii selections, Pink Autumn Sage is a highly effective hummingbird and butterfly plant — the tubular flower structure and nectar production attract Ruby-throated Hummingbirds and a variety of butterflies throughout the growing season. The combination of drought tolerance, extended bloom, wildlife value, and reliable performance in the dry, calcareous soils of zone 7–9 makes this one of the most practical flowering perennials for water-wise gardens in the region.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Salvia greggii Selections for Texas
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Salvia greggii Native Plant Profile
University of Texas San Antonio Native Plant Society — Pink Autumn Sage Cultivation
